item: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.
notes: Both agents consistently oppose the statement from first to final turn. Agent A argues that common-good language can silence dissent, excuse corruption, pressure minorities, and protect the powerful. Agent B directly responds to public order and common-good points while maintaining that individual rights must be a real boundary. Both concede only narrow limits for public order, safety, or accountable rules; no visible flip or directional drift appears.
